title: Molecular Analysis of Trophic Interactions for Ecological Network Construction
author: Jordan P Cuff

steps.instruction: Consider what the experimental aims are and decide sampling methods accordingly. The methods we present below would, for example, be relevant to projects investigating the predator-prey interactions of spiders, the omnivorous interactions of beetles or parasitism rates in herbivorous insects, but could extend beyond these examples into other sample and interaction types. Some of the sample types that could be used with this protocol. For some (e.g., faeces) additional reagents may help to reduce the prevalence of inhibitors that can interfere with PCR, which will be covered in other purpose-built protocols. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m The following steps of the protocol are based on a small (< 10 mg ) sample of tissue (e.g., invertebrate guts). The protocol will likely work for other sample types, including faeces, soil and biofilms, but it is recommended to look at similar magnetic bead based DNA extraction protocols to add any components that reduce the prevalence or effect of PCR inhibitors (e.g., addition of flocculant solution following lysis).
Add 200 μL of the supernatant from STEP 10 into each well containing Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol.

Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol master mix should comprise: A B C 1 Reagent Amount per sample Amount per 96-well plate 2 Protein Denaturation Buffer 220 μL 42.2 mL 3 Ethanol (100 %) 220 μL 42.2 μL

Add all of the sample solution (~ 600 μL) to a well in a 96-well DNA membrane spin column and cover with a breathable seal. Ensure there is a suitable reservoir beneath into which the flow-through will go (e.g., 2.2 mL deep-well plate).
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 10 minutes) will work.
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work.

Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 7 M 95.53 29.4 g 3 Ethanol 56 % - 56 mL 4 H2O - - 44 mL
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

Wash Buffer 2 should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Ethanol (100 %) 70 % 58.44 70 mL 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 4 H2O - - 29 mL
Centrifuge at 20,000 x g for 3 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 20,000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 15 minutes) will work.

Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0.5 mL DNA collection plate. The liquid level following the final wash will be close to the base of the spin column, so take care not to let it touch to prevent ethanol transfer to the soon-to-be eluted DNA.
Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly to the silica membrane and leave it at room temperature for 5 minutes, covering with a breathable seal.

Elution Buffer should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 3 H2O - - 99 mL

Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute. The DNA is now in the collection plate and can be taken forward to amplification. Steps 20-21 can be repeated for increased DNA yield but a lower overall concentration. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work, although should not be necessary for this step.
Add 400 μL of master mix of Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol (detailed below) to each well of the 2.2 ml 96-well plate.

Protein Denaturation Buffer should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 5 M 95.53 47.7 g 3 H2O - - 100 mL

Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol master mix should comprise: A B C 1 Reagent Amount per sample Amount per 96-well plate 2 Protein Denaturation Buffer 220 μL 21.1 mL 3 Ethanol (100 %) 220 μL 21.1 μL

Add all of the sample solution (~ 600 μL) to a well in a 96-well silica membrane spin column and cover with a breathable seal. Ensure there is a suitable reservoir beneath into which the flow-through will go (e.g., 2.2 mL deep-well plate).
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 10 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 10 minutes) will work.
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 7 M 95.53 29.4 g 3 Ethanol 56 % - 56 mL 4 H2O - - 44 mL
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 5 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work.
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0.5 mL DNA collection plate. The liquid level following the final wash will be close to the base of the spin column, so take care not to let it touch to prevent ethanol transfer to the soon-to-be eluted DNA.

Wash Buffer 2 should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Ethanol (100 %) 70 % 58.44 70 mL 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 4 H2O - - 29 mL
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 15 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 20,000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 15 minutes) will work.
Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly to the silica membrane and leave it at room temperature for 5 minutes, covering with a breathable seal.

Elution Buffer should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 3 H2O - - 99 mL

Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 2 minutes. The DNA is now in the collection plate and can be taken forward to amplification. Steps 20-21 can be repeated for increased DNA yield but a lower overall concentration. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work, although should not be necessary for this step.
Grind the larvae in a Geno/Grinder 2010 at full speed (1750 RPM) for 4 minutes. If the larvae are not fully homogenised, repeat this step.
Centrifuge at 4,000 g for 2 minutes.
Remove the ball bearings using a magnet, taking care not to cross-contaminate the samples.
To the lysate, add 1 mL of freshly-prepared Proteinase Buffer master mix of Lysis Buffer 2 and proteinase K (detailed in the sub-steps below) and vortex to mix.

Lysis Buffer 2 should be pH 9 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Sodium chloride 120 mM 58.44 0.701 g 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 50 mM - 5 mL 4 0.5 M EDTA 20 mM - 4 mL 5 SDS 3 % - 3 g 6 H2O - - 91 mL

substeps.instruction: Lysis Buffer 2 should be pH 9 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Sodium chloride 120 mM
M EDTA 20 mM - 4 mL 5 SDS 3 % - 3 g 6 H2O - - 91 mL

steps.instruction: Per sample, the Proteinase Buffer master mix should comprise: A B 1 Reagent Amount per plate 2 Lysis solution 2 102.4 mL 3 Proteinase K (10 mg/mL) 3.168 mL
Incubate at 37 °C overnight (12-16 hours) All proteins including Thaumetopoeitin are now inactivated.
Centrifuge at 4000 x g for 4 minutes.
Transfer 1 mL of the supernatant to a clean 2.2 mL deep-well 96-well plate for archiving/backup and retain the rest in the original plate.
Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 4 minutes.
Transfer 200 μL of the supernatant to a clean 2.2 mL deep-well 96-well plate. The remaining lysate can now be stored at -20 °C for backup/future work.

Add one 3 mm hardened carbon steel bead to each well. Beads are usually shipped coated in manufacturing oil (especially the carbon steel beads). To remove this, place beads in a borosilicate glass beaker or Duran bottle with plastic pouring lip and lid removed then bake for at least 12 hours at 250 °C.

Using sterilised forceps, prepare each sample for molecular analysis by carefully and gently clearing away any non-target tissue (e.g., non-gut tissue if extracting DNA from gut contents) with ethanol and/or a sterile scalpel to reduce the prevalence of non-target (e.g., predator) DNA downstream. Between each sample, thoroughly decontaminate dissection tools and any surfaces used (e.g., Petri dish) with Chemgene (or a similar disinfectant), rinsing with molecular biology-grade water and 100 % ethanol, and wiping dry with sterile tissue. If pausing before proceeding to homogenisation and lysis, seal the 96-well plate and store it at -20 °C until ready. When subsequently defrosting it, check that the TNES does not precipitate. If it does, gently warm the plate to 50-60 °C .

Transfer individual samples into 96-well plates (ideally deep-well, e.g., 2.2 mL, with fixable lids for grinding/washing). Consider the distribution of experimental controls ahead of subsequent steps to streamline downstream liquid handling. Our recommended PCR plate layout, which could be adopted here, during DNA extraction, for streamlining further into the protocol (i.e., to avoid having to redistribute samples across plates). Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m Store samples at -20 °C until ready to process.
Centrifuge the plate at 2000 x g, Room temperature, 2 minutes .

Add 100 µL TNES lysis buffer to each well of a 96-well plate. For the TNES buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 100 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 52 millimolar (mM) NaCl, 10 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 10 Mass / % volume SDS).
Grind the samples in a tissue grinder/homogeniser/lyser at 1750 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute .

Prepare Kingfisher Apex reagent plates as below. The Kingfisher Apex is ideal for automated high-throughput extraction of DNA, but will not always be available. Alternative equipment can achieve similar results, including just using magnetic racks. In that case, rather than transferring beads between plates, remove the supernatant and replace it with the next reagent, as described for normalisation below.
Add 10 µL 20 mg/mL papain to each well.
Incubate overnight (~ 16:00:00 ) at 37 °C or 02:00:00 at 56 °C .
Insert plates into the Kingfisher Apex and run a preset programme with the below steps.

Using tagged primers, unique identifiers can be established for a full plate with 8 uniquely tagged forward primers and 12 uniquely tagged reverse primers. For ease, if able to use a 96-well pipette, consider creating a "primer plate" containing both PCR primers for each well at 5 micromolar (µM) concentration; this is especially effective when using multiple plates. For 25 µL reaction volumes, this will subsequently involve adding 22.75 µL hot-start Taq polymerase and water master mix (described in the step above) to each well, followed by 1.25 µL of each primer mix to its corresponding well. It is possible to do this with a multichannel pipette. Distribution of tagged PCR primers across the 96-well plate. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m
Add 1 µL DNA to each corresponding sample or positive control well, and 1 µL molecular grade water to each negative control other than extraction negative control(s).
Briefly centrifuge the plate to ensure that the oil is above the PCR mix and everything is at the bottom of each well without air bubbles.

Load the PCR plate into a thermocycler. Ensure that the temperature regime matches the enzyme used (including any heat activation for hot-start Taq) and that the annealing temperature matches the PCR primers used. Given differences between labs and samples, and inaccuracies in temperature calibration, considering running a temperature gradient PCR with known samples to check the specificity of your PCR primers.

For the sample plate, to each well of a 96-well deep-well plate, add 60 µL of the lysate from STEP 11, 120 µL of 1.5X GITC buffer, 120 µL of 1 mg/mL SeraMag Speed Beads in TE ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris and 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA) and 240 µL isopropanol. For the 1.5X GITC buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 6 Molarity (M) GITC, 75 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 3 % volume sarkosyl, 30 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.15 % volume antifoam).
For the two ethanol plates, to each well of two 96-well deep-well plates, add 400 µL 80 % ethanol.
For the isopropanol plate, to each well of a 96-well deep-well plate, add 400 µL isopropanol.
For the elution plate, to each well of a 96-well standard plate, add 100 µL molecular biology grade water.
Pick up the 96 deep-well tip comb from a 96-well standard plate.
Bind DNA to the beads by mixing at medium speed for 00:05:00 with a slow post-mix for 00:05:00 .
Collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into the isopropanol plate and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into one of the 80 % ethanol plates and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into one of the 80 % ethanol plates and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Dry the beads above the well for 00:02:00 .
Release the beads into the elution plate and mix for 00:02:00 at fast speed with a slow post-mix for 00:03:00 , and collect the beads in four 00:00:20 collections.
Leave the tip comb in an empty 96-well standard plate.
Store the eluted DNA at -20 °C until ready for subsequent steps.

Decide how samples will be distributed across plates (but don't distribute the DNA yet). Consider including a negative control in each row and column to detect any contaminants in each tagged forward and reverse primer. Among these wells, include any DNA extraction negative controls. Include positive controls (ideally mixed samples of species not found in the same study system), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). Include blank controls (ideally wells into which no reagents or at least no primers are added), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). If using multiple PCR primer pairs, familiarise yourself with the annealing temperatures for each and prepare separate PCR plates for each. For optimal accuracy, consider running replicates of each reaction (e.g., triplicates). Our recommended PCR plate layout, which could be adopted here for streamlining downstream. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m

Prepare enough PCR mastermix for each sample. For a full plate, the below values will usually suffice (with some overage to account for pipetting error), but check your specific Taq polymerase mix for any differences: %s %s These values are for 25 µL reaction volumes, which have been demonstrated to be effective for community metabarcoding. Consider running them in triplicate for more accurate results.

Distribute one drop of mineral oil into each well of the PCR plate(s) (~ 20 µL ). This can be achieved by taking a large volume of mineral oil into the pipette tip and then gently depressing the plunger so that a drop forms and falls from the tip into each well.Mineral oil improves sealing of reactions by preventing evaporation and condensation. By reducing evaporation and thus loss of product, this also reduces potential cross-contamination.

The samples should now be checked for successful amplification, contamination in negatives and any secondary banding. Gel electrophoresis will achieve this, but digital systems like the Qiagen Qiaxcel will do this and facilitate equalisation by generating amplicon-specific DNA concentations.
Run your PCR programme.

The DNA extraction protocol is largely adapted from the BOMB-Bio tissue nucleic acid extraction protocol. See their documentation for additional detail. Bio-On-Magnetic-Beads (BOMB): Open platform for high-throughput nucleic acid extraction and manipulation..

Analyse the PCR products via digital electrophoresis (e.g., Qiagen Qiaxcel). This provides data on the concentration of specific amplicons (i.e., the target amplicon of the PCR primers used), which will be used in the next two steps. If digital electrophoresis (or an equivalent platform that facilitates quantification of concentration by band size) isn't available, you could proceed through the steps from STEP 31 in this section and quantify concentration using a fluorescence-based assay (e.g., Qubit dsDNA assay) and pool (as in STEPS 29-30) based on those values. It is important that primer dimers and other small fragments are removed (e.g., via magnetic bead-based purification) prior to these fluorescence-based assays as they can otherwise inflate and distort concentration values.

Based on the concentration of the target amplicon in each sample, calculate how much to pool from each sample in order to achieve equimolarity (i.e., equal representation of samples). This can be achieved by dividing the maximum concentration within the plate by the concentration of each individual sample. The negatives, blanks and low concentration samples (for which the amount to pool exceeds the total volume available) should be pooled differently. This is summarised in STEP 26.

Pool the value calculated for each sample in the previous step as a volume in microlitres. Positive controls can be treated as samples. For negative controls, pool the average volume pooled across the samples (i.e., calculate the amount to be pooled from each sample to achieve equimolarity, calculate the mean of all of these values for each plate, and then use this value as the amount to pool from each negative control). For blanks, do not pool anything. If the volume to be pooled exceeds the total volume available, pool the total volume available. For samples with low or no concentration, the PCRs for these samples could be repeated, the samples could be excluded, or these can be pooled in the same manner as negative controls (although be wary in any interpretation of the data given increased potential for inaccuracy). If most of the volumes are similar and small, you can double (or otherwise multiply) the values. If there is a large variation in the volumes to be pooled, the values can be halved to facilitate pooling more dilute samples proportionally.

To purify these pools ahead of library preparation, prepare a 1X solid phase reversible immobilisation (SPRI) bead solution and bring it to room temperature. The below steps detail how to make this solution, but it is also commercially available.

If using beads such as Sera-Mag Magnetic SpeedBeads (carboxylated, 1 µm, 3 EDAC/PA5), take 1 mL of well-mixed bead solution and wash the beads twice with TE+Tween buffer ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris base, 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.05 % volume Tween 20, pH 8.0) by magnetising the beads, removing the supernatant, adding the TE+Tween, remagnetising the beads and removing the supernatant, and repeating the addition and removal of TE+Tween once more.
To the beads, add the following mix: %s %s
Add 20 mL of 50 % volume PEG to the tube to reach a 1X bead solution.

If ≥ 20 µL of PCR product is available for each pool, pipette 20 µL of 1X SPRI bead solution into each corresponding well of a 96-well plate or individual tubes. If less DNA is available, add a volume of SPRI bead solution equivalent to the full available volume of PCR product to the each corresponding well of the new 96-well plate or individual tubes. When working with magnetic bead solutions, ensure they are at room temperature and fully mixed, with no residue at the bottom of the container. This is a good opportunity for size selection as well, especially if the PCR product contains any secondary bands. If using SPRI beads, adjust the volume added to select different fragment sizes. Refer to manufacturer details for more information on size selection. Larger volumes can be used, so long as the corresponding volume of beads is used. For volumes greater than 100 µL , consider doubling the ethanol and water volumes in the remaining steps of this section.

Add 20 µL of PCR product (or whatever volume of beads was used in the last step) to each corresponding well of 1X bead solution, avoiding oil, and mix by vortexing ( 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute ). To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .

Remove all but 5 µL of the mixture from each well via pipette without disturbing the beads, which should be settled on the magnet. We recommend leaving 5 µL behind simply to avoid pipetting the beads themselves, but this can be avoided with care and experience.
Add 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.
Remove the ethanol and add a further 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.

Remove the ethanol as completely as possible by pipetting and allow the beads to air-dry until the aggregation of magnetic beads transitions from 'glossy' (shiny reflection of light) to 'matte' (dull dark brown mass), but not so long that it dries completely (i.e., begins to turn a rusty red and shows cracks).
Add 11 µL molecular grade water to each well, shake at 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute and incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Remove 10 µL of the supernatant and place it into a new tube. This will be the library used in any subsequent library preparation.

Quantify the concentration of the library from the previous step (e.g., using Qubit dsDNA assay) and take this forward for library preparation. For Oxford Nanopore Technologies sequencing, follow the manufacturer protocols (or adaptations of them on here). For Illumina sequencing, consider protocols such as the one this is forked from if your primers include a Nextera overhang; otherwise, consider ligation-based library preparation methods. Check with your sequencing provider how many fmol they will need in how many µL. This will be based on the sequencer, sequencing cartridge and any QC processes they follow.

Equalisation is more effective than normalisation, but requires amplicon concentration data. Both can be time and labour intensive though, so can be skipped if time restricted for large-scale projects, although at the expense of data recovery and evenness. This protocol presents equalisation, but see the protocol from which this is forked for a step-by-step description of normalisation. If the PCRs were replicated (i.e., each sample run multiple times for each PCR primer pair used), these can be merged together into one plate at this point, or carried forward separately. Keeping the replicates separate increases the number of libraries to prepare and sequence later, but better facilitates identification of inconsistencies between samples that may arise from contamination or error. To merge triplicates, assuming use of 25 µL reaction volumes, pipette 22 µL from each well of two of the three plates into the corresponding well of the third. Briefly centrifuge the merged plate to move the oil to the top of the product again. To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.
Based on the equipment available, decide whether to follow the robotics-based or standard protocol using the step-case options below.

To a new plate, or to individual tubes (depending on the compatibility of plasticware and magnetic tube/plate racks), add 60 µL of the lysate from STEP 10, 120 µL of 1.5X GITC buffer, 120 µL of 1 mg/mL SeraMag Speed Beads in TE ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris and 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA) and 240 µL isopropanol. Mix by pipetting or vortexing 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute . For the 1.5X GITC buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 6 Molarity (M) GITC, 75 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 3 % volume sarkosyl, 30 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.15 % volume antifoam). When working with magnetic bead solutions, ensure they are at room temperature and fully mixed, with no residue at the bottom of the container.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .

Remove all but 5 µL of the mixture from each well via pipette without disturbing the beads, which should be settled on the magnet. We recommend leaving 5 µL behind simply to avoid pipetting the beads themselves, but this can be avoided with care and experience.
Add 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.
Remove the ethanol entirely and discard it.

Remove the ethanol as completely as possible by pipetting and discard it. Allow the beads to air-dry until the aggregation of magnetic beads transitions from 'glossy' (shiny reflection of light) to 'matte' (dull dark brown mass), but not so long that it dries completely (i.e., begins to turn a rusty red and shows cracks).
Add 105 µL molecular grade water to each well, shake at 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute and incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Remove 100 µL of the supernatant and place it into a new tube. This will be the sample used for PCR.
Add 400 µL isopropanol to each well.
Remove the isopropanol entirely and discard it.
Add a further 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.
Store the eluted DNA at -20 °C until ready for subsequent steps.

Decide how samples will be distributed across plates (but don't distribute the DNA yet). Consider including a negative control in each row and column to detect any contaminants in each tagged forward and reverse primer. Among these wells, include any DNA extraction negative controls. Include positive controls (ideally mixed samples of species not found in the same study system), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). Include blank controls (ideally wells into which no reagents or at least no primers are added), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). If using multiple PCR primer pairs, familiarise yourself with the annealing temperatures for each and prepare separate PCR plates for each. For optimal accuracy, consider running replicates of each reaction (e.g., triplicates). Our recommended PCR plate layout, which could be adopted here for streamlining downstream. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m

Prepare enough PCR mastermix for each sample. For a full plate, the below values will usually suffice (with some overage to account for pipetting error), but check your specific Taq polymerase mix for any differences: %s %s These values are for 25 µL reaction volumes, which have been demonstrated to be effective for community metabarcoding. Consider running them in triplicate for more accurate results.

Using tagged primers, unique identifiers can be established for a full plate with 8 uniquely tagged forward primers and 12 uniquely tagged reverse primers. For ease, if able to use a 96-well pipette, consider creating a "primer plate" containing both PCR primers for each well at 5 micromolar (µM) concentration; this is especially effective when using multiple plates. For 25 µL reaction volumes, this will subsequently involve adding 22.75 µL hot-start Taq polymerase and water master mix (described in the step above) to each well, followed by 1.25 µL of each primer mix to its corresponding well. It is possible to do this with a multichannel pipette. Distribution of tagged PCR primers across the 96-well plate. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m
Add 1 µL DNA to each corresponding sample or positive control well, and 1 µL molecular grade water to each negative control other than extraction negative control(s).

Distribute one drop of mineral oil into each well of the PCR plate(s) (~ 20 µL ). This can be achieved by taking a large volume of mineral oil into the pipette tip and then gently depressing the plunger so that a drop forms and falls from the tip into each well.Mineral oil improves sealing of reactions by preventing evaporation and condensation. By reducing evaporation and thus loss of product, this also reduces potential cross-contamination.
Briefly centrifuge the plate to ensure that the oil is above the PCR mix and everything is at the bottom of each well without air bubbles.

Load the PCR plate into a thermocycler. Ensure that the temperature regime matches the enzyme used (including any heat activation for hot-start Taq) and that the annealing temperature matches the PCR primers used. Given differences between labs and samples, and inaccuracies in temperature calibration, considering running a temperature gradient PCR with known samples to check the specificity of your PCR primers.
Run your PCR programme.

The samples should now be checked for successful amplification, contamination in negatives and any secondary banding. Gel electrophoresis will achieve this, but digital systems like the Qiagen Qiaxcel will do this and facilitate equalisation by generating amplicon-specific DNA concentrations (see the robotics-based protocol step-case).

Equalisation is more effective than normalisation, but requires amplicon concentration data. Both can be time and labour intensive though, so can be skipped if time restricted for large-scale projects, although at the expense of data recovery and evenness. The robotics-based protocol step-case goes through equalisation using a Qiagen Qiaxcel, but this process could also be emulated by bead-cleaning all of the samples individually (as per the bead clean in the equalisation protocol following the pooling steps), and then using a Qubit fluorometer (or similar) to quantify the concentrations of amplicons. Samples could then be pooled based on Qubit concentrations, and will then be ready for library preparation (no further bead cleaning necessary unless the overall concentration is low and you would like to concentrate the library by eluting into a smaller volume). If doing that, it is crucial that the Qubit measurements are taken after bead cleaning so that primers and primer dimers do not contribute to the DNA concentrations used for pooling. If the PCRs were replicated (i.e., each sample run multiple times for each PCR primer pair used), these can be merged together into one plate at this point, or carried forward separately. Keeping the replicates separate increases the number of libraries to prepare and sequence later, but better facilitates inconsistencies between samples. To merge triplicates, assuming use of 10 µL reaction volumes, pipette 8 µL from each well of two of the three plates into the corresponding well of the third. Briefly centrifuge the merged plate to move the oil to the top of the product again. To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.
Prepare 0.1X SPRI bead solution and bring to room temperature. The below steps detail how to make this solution, but it is also commercially available.

If ≥ 20 µL of PCR product is available in each well, pipette 20 µL of 0.1X SPRI bead solution into each well of a new 96-well plate. If less DNA is available, add the available volume of 0.1X SPRI beads and add the same volume of PCR product to these beads in the next step. When working with magnetic beads, ensure they are fully mixed with no residue at the bottom of the container.

If using beads such as Sera-Mag Magnetic SpeedBeads (carboxylated, 1 µm, 3 EDAC/PA5), take 1 mL of well-mixed bead solution and wash the beads twice with TE+Tween buffer ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris base, 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.05 % volume Tween 20, pH 8.0) by magnetising the beads, removing the supernatant, adding the TE+Tween, remagnetising the beads and removing the supernatant, and repeating the addition and removal of TE+Tween once more.
To the beads, add the following mix: A B 1 Reagent Volume 2 5 M NaCl 25 mL 3 Molecular grade water 3.582 mL 4 1 N HCl 0.168 mL 5 1 M Tris base 0.5 mL 6 0.1 M disodium EDTA 0.5 mL

substeps.instruction: To the beads, add the following mix: A B 1 Reagent Volume 2 5 M NaCl 25 mL 3 Molecular grade water

steps.instruction: Add 20 mL of 50 % (v/v) PEG to the tube to reach a 1X bead solution (alongside making the 0.1X solution, this will be useful later).

Add 5 mL of 1X bead solution to 45 mL of the following mix to make a 0.1X solution: A B 1 Reagent Volume 2 5 M NaCl 25 mL 3 Molecular grade water 3.582 mL 4 1 N HCl 0.168 mL 5 1 M Tris base 0.5 mL 6 0.1 M disodium EDTA 0.5 mL 7 50 % PEG 20 mL

substeps.instruction: Add 5 mL of 1X bead solution to 45 mL of the following mix to make a 0.1X solution: A B 1 Reagent Volume 2 5 M NaCl 25 mL 3 Molecular grade water

steps.instruction: Add 20 µL of PCR product (or whatever volume of beads was used in the last step) to each corresponding well of 0.1X beads, avoiding oil, and mix by vortexing ( 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute ). To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Remove all but 5 µL of the mixture from each well via pipette without disturbing the beads, which should be settled on the magnet (although can be hard to see with 0.1X solutions).
Add 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.
Remove the ethanol and add a further 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.

Remove the ethanol as completely as possible by pipetting. A small amount of remaining ethanol isn't debilitating in this case, unlike usual magnetic bead cleaning protocols, because we will complete a subsequent purification immediately after.
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Add 10 µL molecular grade water to each well, shake at 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute and incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .

Pool 8 µL from each well into a single tube, leaving magnetic beads behind in the 96-well plate. To pool samples using a multi-channel pipette, pipette each column of samples into a new strip of 200 µL tubes, then merge all of these tubes into a single 1.5 mL tube at the end.
Mix the pooled samples by flicking and inverting, and transfer 700 µL into a new 2.5 mL microcentrifuge tube.

Add 700 µL 1X magnetic beads for DNA purification (see note below for size selection) and mix by vortexing. Incubate for 00:05:00 . This is a good opportunity for size selection as well, especially if the PCR product contains any secondary bands. If using SPRI beads, adjust the volume added to select different fragment sizes.
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Add 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol.
Remove the ethanol and add a further 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol.

Remove the ethanol completely. Briefly centrifuge the sample to collect and remove any residual ethanol. Allow to air-dry until the aggregation of magnetic beads transitions from 'glossy' (shiny reflection of light) to 'matte' (dull dark brown mass), but not so long that it dries completely (i.e., begins to turn a rusty red and shows cracks).
Add 100 µL molecular grade water and mix by vortexing. Incubate for 00:05:00 at Room temperature .
Place the sample on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Remove 95 µL of the water and place it in a new tube. This will be the library used in the subsequent section.

Quantify the concentration of the library from the previous step (e.g., using Qubit dsDNA assay) and take this forward for library preparation. For Oxford Nanopore Technologies sequencing, follow the manufacturer protocols (or adaptations of them on here). For Illumina sequencing, consider protocols such as the one this is forked from if your primers include a Nextera overhang; otherwise, consider ligation-based library preparation methods. Check with your sequencing provider how many fmol they will need in how many µL. This will be based on the sequencer, sequencing cartridge and any QC processes they follow.
